Preparation of Mesoporous High-Surface-Area
Activated Carbon**
By Zhonghua Hu,* Madapusi P. Srinivasan, and
Yaming Ni
Activated carbons (ACs) are usually microporous mate-
rials. Their main application is as adsorbents for the purifi-
cation of gases and water, and chemical processing. Ac-
cording to the International Union of Pure and Applied
Chemistry (IUPAC), the pores of a porous material can be
classified into three groups: micropores (diameter, d <
2 nm), mesopores (2 nm < d < 50 nm), and macropores
(d > 50 nm). For typical activated carbons, surface areas
are 800±1500 m2/g, mesopore surface areas are in the range
of 10±200 m2/g, and mesopore volumes vary between 0.1
and 0.2 cm3/g, but higher values (up to 0.5 cm3/g) can be
found in special cases.[1] The maximum of the pore volume
distribution curve versus the pore width (diameter) is
mostly in the range of 0.4±2.0 nm. The process of filling
mesopores, also known as transitional pores, with adsorb-
ate takes places via the mechanism of capillary condensa-
tion. Therefore, mesopores contribute significantly to ad-
sorption, but also act as the main transport arteries for the
adsorbate. In recent years, porous carbon materials have
been used as catalytic materials, battery electrode materi-
als, capacitor materials, gas storage materials, and biomedi-
cal engineering materials.[2] For such applications, the car-
bon materials should have a high mesopore content, so that
large molecules and ions that are too large to enter micro-
pores can penetrate and be adsorbed efficiently in such
pores. Microporous activated carbons, including ordinary
AC, high-surface-area (HSA) AC, and carbon molecular
sieves (CMS), have been extensively reported in the litera-
ture. In contrast, mesoporous activated carbons or meso-
porous activated carbon fibers have been reported only re-
cently.[2±9]
These
mesoporous
carbons,
prepared
via
complicated and high-cost processes, usually have a low or
moderate surface area.
CO2 physical activation and ZnCl2 chemical activation
are two common methods for the production of activated
carbon. It is also possible to combine the two pro-
cesses.[10,11] It was found that a simultaneous physical and
_______________________
±
[*] Prof. Z. Hu, Prof. Y. Ni
Department of Chemistry
Tongji University
1239 Siping Road, Shanghai 200 092 (China)
Dr. M. P. Srinivasan
Department of Chemical Engineering
National University of Singapore
10 Kent Ridge Crescent, 119 260 (Singapore)
[**] This work was supported by the National Science and Technology
Board of Singapore (NSTB) under a grant for the project on Environ-
mental Technology (RP 3 602 037) and the Scientific Research
Foundation for Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, State Education
Ministry (China).
 chemical activation technique helps to enlarge pores, re-
sulting in a conversion of micropores to mesopores.
In this study, we tried to use simultaneous physical and
chemical activation to prepare mesoporous high-surface-
area (MHSA) activated carbons from coconut shell. The
resultant activated carbons possess an N2 Brunauer±Em-
mett±Teller (BET) surface area over 2000 m2/g, a total
pore volume up to 1.9 cm3/g and a mesoporosity of 71 %.
Large adsorption capacities for methylene blue and ery-
throsine red verified the mesoporosity. Such mesoporous
high-surface-area carbons were rarely reported.
The MHSA activated carbons were prepared as follows.
Coconut shells were impregnated with a concentrated solu-
tion of ZnCl2. The mixtures were then dried at 110 C and
heated up to 800 C at a rate of 10 C/min, in a horizontal
furnace under constant flow of nitrogen. The carbons were
activated at 800 C for 2 h in a CO2 atmosphere. The re-
acted products were washed with hydrochloric acid to dis-
solve and remove zinc compounds, and then rinsed with
hot deionized water until free of chloride ions. The result-
ing activated carbon samples were dried in air at 110 C
overnight.
The N2 adsorption±desorption isotherms of the carbons
were measured using an automatic adsorption instrument
(Quantachrome Corp., NOVA-1000 gas sorption analyzer)
to determine surface area and pore volume. The surface
area was calculated using the BET equation,[12] which is the
most widely used model for determining the specific sur-
face area. However, this model is not very suitable for mi-
croporous materials such as activated carbons. Thus, it
should be noted that the calculated BET surface area does
not represent the true surface area of the activated carbons,
and is therefore called an apparent surface area. The meso-
porous surface area and micropore volume were estimated
using the t-plot.[12] The pore size distribution was calculated
using the Barrett±Joyner±Halenda (BJH) method.[12] The
mesopore volume was obtained by subtracting the micro-
pore volume from the total pore volume. The total pore
volume (micropore volume + mesopore volume) was esti-
mated to be the liquid volume of nitrogen at a relative
pressure of about 0.99.
Figure 1 shows nitrogen adsorption isotherms of acti-
vated carbons prepared at different ZnCl2 to shell ratios. It
is clear that the shape of the isotherms changes gradually
with increasing activating agent ZnCl2. The carbons pre-
pared with low ZnCl2/shell ratios exhibit a steep type I iso-
therm, according to IUPAC classification. Major uptake oc-
curs at low relative pressures of less than 0.1, resulting in
an almost horizontal plateau at higher relative pressures.
This is indicative of highly microporous materials with a
narrow pore size distribution (pore diameters are relatively
uniform). As the ZnCl2/shell ratio increases, the gradual in-
crease in nitrogen uptake and larger adsorbed volume
(higher horizontal plateau) results in a less steep isotherm
of type I, suggesting that additional pores were created and
small pores were widened. Furthermore, as the ratio ex-
ceeds 2, the increase in nitrogen uptake is sustained
throughout the entire pressure range so that the isotherms
take up a shape resembling a combination of type I and II.
Additionally, the hysteretic loops of desorption become
pronounced, indicating the development of mesoporosity.
Such shapes of isotherms were rarely reported for activated
carbons. Conventional ACs show a typical type I isotherm,
which is characterized by steep adsorption at low relative
pressures and a very small increase in adsorption at high
relative pressures.
Table 1 lists the porosity parameters of the carbon sam-
ples calculated according to the BET equation, the t-plot,
and the BJH method from the nitrogen adsorption±desorp-
tion isotherms at 77 K. It is evident that the BET surface
area increases with increasing ratio of ZnCl2/shell from
0.25 to 2, and then decreases slightly. However, the meso-
porous surface area increases continually with the ratios in-
vestigated. The increment of mesoporous surface area is
more pronounced when the ratio is higher than 2. The sam-
ples CZ7 and CZ8 possess a mesopore surface area as high
as 1046 m2/g and 1208 m2/g, respectively; the values sub-
stantially exceed those expected for conventional activated
carbons. The total pore volume of activated carbon de-
pends greatly on the ratio. The greater the amount of agent
ZnCl2 present, the larger the pore volume is. Again, the
samples CZ7 and CZ8 have an extremely large total pore
volume of 1.748 cm3/g and 1.913 cm3/g, respectively (about

Preparation of Mesoporous High-Surface-Area

AC, high-surface-area (HSA) AC, and carbon molecular

vated carbons prepared at different ZnCl2 to shell ratios. It

with increasing activating agent ZnCl2. The carbons pre-

therm, according to IUPAC classification. Major uptake oc-
curs at low relative pressures of less than 0.1, resulting in
an almost horizontal plateau at higher relative pressures.
This is indicative of highly microporous materials with a
narrow pore size distribution (pore diameters are relatively
uniform). As the ZnCl2/shell ratio increases, the gradual in-

(higher horizontal plateau) results in a less steep isotherm
of type I, suggesting that additional pores were created and

ceeds 2, the increase in nitrogen uptake is sustained
throughout the entire pressure range so that the isotherms

pronounced, indicating the development of mesoporosity.

ples calculated according to the BET equation, the t-plot,
and the BJH method from the nitrogen adsorption±desorp-
tion isotherms at 77 K. It is evident that the BET surface
area increases with increasing ratio of ZnCl2/shell from
0.25 to 2, and then decreases slightly. However, the meso-
porous surface area increases continually with the ratios in-

0.5 cm3/g for typical activated carbons). Whereas, the mi-
cropore volume increases as the ratio of ZnCl2/shell in-
creases up to 2, and then decreases significantly, in contrast,
the mesopore volume increases slightly at low ratios, and
increases dramatically as the ratio exceeds 2. The values of
mesopore volume can be as high as 1.164 cm3/g and
1.364 cm3/g for CZ7 and CZ8, respectively. As a result, the
mesoporosity (the percentage of mesopore to total pore
volume, Vme/Vtot) changes enormously from 28 % to 67 %
as the ratio varies from 2 to 2.5. Furthermore, it reaches a
value of 71 % at a ZnCl2/shell ratio of 3. The increase of to-
tal pore volume accompanied by the decrease in micropore
volume, suggesting an increase in mesopores at the expense
of micropores, is similar to the results of Hussein et al.[11]
The plots of the ZnCl2/shell ratio versus pore volume
clearly display the changes in pore volume as shown in Fig-
ure 2. The change in total pore volume appears to be linear
for the ratios investigated. The micropore volume increases
gradually up to a ratio of 2. However, the higher ZnCl2/shell
ratios cause a significant decrease
in micropore volume. As for the
mesopore volume, three different
regions exist: the mesopore vol-
ume increases, either very little or
if at all, for those ratios lower than
1; in the ratio range of 1±2, it in-
creases gradually; and when the
ratios are higher than 2 the meso-
cantly. This suggests that the acti-
creates more new pores, but also
widens the pores, so that both con-
tributions cause the development
of mesoporosity. This is also reflected in the average pore
size, as shown in the last column of Table 1. The average
pore sizes of sample CZ7 and CZ8 were enlarged to 3.15 nm
and 3.49 nm, respectively. Although all the samples are
highly porous, their pore sizes are different. Mesopores are
dominant for samples CZ7 and CZ8, i.e., the two samples
are highly porous with a high mesopore content. In contrast,
micropores are dominant for the other samples.
Figure 3 shows the pore size distributions of samples
CZ5, CZ6, CZ7, CZ8, and Filtrasorb 100. The maxima of
the distribution curves occur at less than 2 nm for samples
made with ZnCl2/shell ratios of less than 1.5, and also for
the case of Filtrasorb100. However, CZ5 exhibits a second
small peak at about 3 nm, indicating incipient mesoporos-
ity. The maximum peak shifts gradually from 2 to 3.49 nm
as the ratio increases from 1 to 3. The pore size distribution
of all the samples prepared in this study is very narrow
(Fig. 3). There are only a few pores larger than 5 nm, espe-
cially for the samples made with low ZnCl2/shell ratios.
The narrow pore size distribution should be very interest-
ing for some applications, such as separation of molecules
of different molecular size. Therefore, these carbon materi-
als could represent a compromise between microporous

zeolites (very narrow pore size distribution, but with a pore
size in the range 0.2±1.2 nm[13]) and mesoporous silica and
alumina (larger mesopores, wide distribution, and surface
areas much lower than those of activated carbons).
Figure 4 shows the adsorption isotherms of phenol (mol-
ecule size <10 ), methylene blue (15 ), and erythrosine
red (19 ) on sample CZ8 and commercial sample Filtra-
sorb100. The difference in the uptake between two carbons
becomes significant with increasing size of the adsorbate
molecule. The adsorption capacity for phenol is compar-
able, while that of CZ8 for methylene blue and erythrosine
is respectively about two and six times higher than that of
Filtrasorb100. Since only pore openings larger than the
adsorbate are accessible to the adsorbate, CZ8 has a much
larger amount of mesopore compared to Filtrasorb100 (the
most effective adsorption takes place on the surface of
those pores that have openings about two times larger than
the size of the adsorbate). On comparing pore volumes
(Table 1) and phenol uptake, it can be seen that the micro-
pore volume of CZ8 and its adsorption capacity for phenol
are slightly higher than those of Filtrasorb100. Since phenol
is a small molecule, the adsorption occurs mainly in the mi-
cropores. Thus, the comparable capacity for phenol and the
larger capacity for erythrosine indicate that CZ8 is indeed
an adsorbent of high mesopore content. This also substanti-
ates the observations from the nitrogen adsorption and
pore size distribution results (Fig. 3).
The present investigation provides a simple and cost-ef-
fective method to produce a new type of carbonaceous ad-
sorbent, mesoporous high-surface-area activated carbons,
from coconut shell. By changing the ratio of activating
agent to carbon precursor, it is possible to control the pore
(2±3.49 nm). Adsorption tests in liquid media show that ad-
sorption capacities conform to calculations based on nitro-
gen adsorption isotherms. The mesoporous nature of the
prepared samples was confirmed by the observed capacity
for erythrosine red, which exhibited six times the capacity
of a commercial activated carbon. These new types of acti-
vated carbon may be very interesting candidates for versa-
tile applications, such as water treatment, gas storage, bat-
tery electrodes, and catalyst support.
Final version: September 28, 1999
